03 Statement of Clarity & Alignment - YouTube.mp4
So the first step to building out your clarity compass is to have one single statement that everyone involved can agree to and that you can reference back to if things get a little off track. We call it the statement of clarity and alignment. Now, this might seem silly at first, but it's actually really, really important because not everyone is going to approach this exercise from the exact same perspective. Some might think that goal setting is all that matters, others just want to talk specific projects, and still others will think the entire process is a complete waste of time. But if everyone can agree to this one statement, then it can become the anchoring point that locks people into the process and keeps them engaged. And the statement I'm referring to is this.
We want to build a scalable business where we have a clear advantage over the competition that aligns with our values and makes a dent in our little corner of the universe. I'm going to say this again, and I want you to see if you can pick up on the major elements. Okay, are you ready? Here it is again. We want to build a scalable business where we have a clear advantage over the competition that... aligns with our values and makes a dent. In other words, an impact in our little corner of the universe. Now, the beautiful thing about this statement is that pretty much everyone should agree with it. That's actually why we call it the statement of clarity and alignment. After all, who doesn't want a scalable business?
I mean, even setting aside the financial incentives, you should want to grow if only to serve more people. At a bare minimum, everyone on your team should agree that growing is a good idea, or why the heck are we all here? So the question isn't, should we scale? The question is, what should scale look like for us? So again, we can all agree that we want a business that scales, yes? Okay, good. Then what about clear advantage? Said another way, do we wanna compete in areas where we're gonna just totally get crushed by the competition? Well, of course not, right? That would be entrepreneurial lunacy. We want to compete in the areas where we know we can win. Now, we obviously need to define what...
those areas are, but can we at least agree that it's better to play to our strengths than our weaknesses? Of course we can, right? And your team will be able to as well. Next, we come to... values. And hopefully this one is a no-brainer for all involved. Surely, as we push forward towards our goals, we want to make sure we're living by our values, right? I mean, set another way, we want to make sure we're acting the right way and doing the right things, right? Now, obviously, we need to define what those values are, which we'll get to in a bit, but can we all at least agree that, whatever we're doing, we're gonna make sure it doesn't directly violate our core values? Yes? Okay, great.
And lastly, who doesn't want to make an impact? I mean, can we all agree that we want to make a small little dent in our small little corner of the universe? This business isn't just about enriching us personally, right? Can we all agree that we genuinely care about... our customers and that we want to make their lives a little better and the world just a little bit brighter? Now, your dent may not be solving childhood hunger or launching rockets into Mars in an effort to one day save the entire human race. Now, don't get me wrong, those things are great. But maybe the dent that you want to make is smaller. Maybe you just want to make someone healthier, wealthier, or wiser.
Maybe you just want to make it easier for someone to get their work done or complete what would otherwise be an excruciatingly complicated and frustrating task. Again, I'm not here. To judge the impact or the dent that you want to make. I'm simply here to remind you that you need to make an impact because scaling a business isn't just about capturing value, it's also about creating value. So even if we aren't clear what our impact is, can we at least agree that we need to make an impact? So assuming we all agree to that, then the only questions we need to answer are: what do we mean by a scalable business? What are our competitive advantages? And what are our company's core values? And lastly, what kind of impact do we want to make?
Again, if everyone agrees with the basic premise of the statement of clarity and alignment, then everyone should agree with the need to answer these four critical questions. And fortunately, that is exactly what the Clarity Compass forces teams to do. Because when we say we want to build a scalable business, what we're actually referring to to is our three-year target. And when we say we want to play to our clear advantages, what we're actually referring to are our strategic anchors. Don't worry, we're going to discuss those in a bit. And when we say our goals and actions need to align with our values, what we are of course referring to is our core values. And lastly, when we say we want to make an impact, a dent, what we're really talking about is our company's purpose, our reason why. So the three-year target, strategic anchors, core values, and company purpose. These are the four categories that make up the four points of your clarity compass. So these are the four areas that we're going to be working through in this session and that you are ultimately going to be workshopping with your team. So if you're ready to get started, let's do it.
